published_date: 2026-04-14
video_id: yCR6mUJibjs
url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yCR6mUJibjs
title: The Macro Minute: Are “dumb money” bulls going to leave “smart money” bears behind again?

Happy Tuesday out there, team 42macro.
Your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our Macro Minute for Tuesday,
April 14th, 2026. Hope everyone's having
a great week. So, as always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
are dumb money bulls going to leave
smart money bears behind again?
Our answer is likely.
2026 is already shaping up to be the
fourth consecutive [clears throat] year
in which investor consensus overreacted
to transitory bearish headlines and
failed to get back invested in time to
maximize upside capture in the
subsequent risk-on market regime.
Here are the most important things we
learned today regarding the six key
macro cycles that influence the momentum
and dispersion within and across asset
markets on a trend basis, starting with
the growth cycle. The March NFIB small
business optimism survey data did not
support our paradigm C or productivity
boom themes. The data did, however,
support our jobless recovery theme.
Elsewhere, Aigintic AI was deployed by
10,000-plus engineers at Citi to
redevelop 30-plus years of legacy code
in 2 days, supporting our productivity
boom theme in the process. Recall that
data integrity failures triggered errors
and hundreds of millions in regulatory
fines, prompting CEO Jane Fraser to
commit to the successful remediation
effort.
In terms of the inflation cycle, the
March NFIB small business optimism
survey data and March existing home
sales data supported our cooling housing
and labor theme. March NFIB small
business optimism survey data also
supported our sticky inflation theme
alongside the March PPI data.
Recall that inflation is by far the most
lagging indicator of the business cycle
and is unlikely to durably return to the
Fed's arbitrary 2% target without a
recession or an AI-led productivity
boom. We view the latter outcome as
likely over the long term. All right,
transition to the monetary policy cycle.
The Senate Banking Committee will
convene a confirmation hearing next week
for Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh,
according to the panel's leading
Republican, Tim Scott. Scott did not
specify timing, though Punchbowl reports
the hearing is slated for April 21st,
which is a week from today.
He indicated the Justice Department is
expected to terminate its criminal probe
into the Fed's renovation project within
weeks, thereby enabling Senator Tom
Tillis to support a favorable committee
vote on Warsh. President Trump signaled
indifference to the nomination timing
risk, slight stating in in a February
Fox Business interview, "If it happens,
it happens." We continue to believe
POTUS is setting up Powell to be the
fall guy if anything goes awry in the
economy prior to the midterm elections.
Uh nothing actionable with respect to
our Bayesian priors from the fiscal
policy or liquidity cycles. And then
finally, with respect to the positioning
cycle, Iran is weighing a temporary halt
to the Strait of Hormuz shipments to
avoid provoking a US blockade and
derailing negotiations. This is
consistent with our sanguine take on the
conflict's resolution and is why the
Nasdaq 100 is engaged in its longest
winning streak since 2021, i.e., 10
consecutive gains. US dollar weakened
for seventh consecutive session for the
same reasons.
Transitioning to our 42macro dashboard,
as always, we'll wrap up with a question
from our community. Uh this one's uh
regarding liquidity. Uh it says, "Uh DD,
can you explain why the 42macro view of
global liquidity is so different from
Michael Howell's view of a declining
rate of liquidity, uh putting uh putting
a risk-off regime?" Uh so, um you know,
I'll be honest to say I'm not familiar
with Michael Howell's latest thoughts on
liquidity, but I assume they're uh as
negative as implied by the question. And
I apologize to you, Michael, if if the
the question is uh uh wrong. Uh So, uh
let me just uh quickly go to our uh
global liquidity uh model, which we use
to forecast leading indicators of
private and public sector liquidity
across geographies. According to uh the
42macro global liquidity proxy, which is
the sum of the uh major central bank
balance sheets across the world, these
10 largest um economies in the world,
their central bank balance sheets,
global broad money supply, as well as
global as well as global fiat uh FX
reserves, uh that metric, the sum of
that metric dollar figure,
is trending higher uh across the globe.
It's trending higher in Australia, so we
maintain our liquidity proxies at the
country level as well. Uh it's trending
higher in Brazil. It's trending higher
in China, which is the largest uh pool
of liquidity uh in the world. Uh India
is trending higher as well, and it's
trending higher in the US. So, uh some
large major economies where liquidity is
is continuing to trend higher uh and uh
on a global basis as well. Uh so, if you
look at our 42macro global liquidity uh
model, uh we can see that it's a very
it's a very clearly a key driver of
asset markets. The black line in these
charts uh indicate uh the 42macro global
liquidity proxy on a year-over-year rate
of change basis. The blue line in this
chart here on the right uh shows the
global stock market capitalization on a
year-over-year rate of change basis. The
orange line here in the chart on the
left shows uh Bitcoin on a
year-over-year rate of change basis. And
as you can see, extremely tight
correlation for uh over uh you know,
over a decade here in terms of the our
our liquidity model relative to global
stock market uh stock market and our
liquidity model relative to Bitcoin,
with the exception of this very
conspicuous divergence that we've
observed since the fall of last year.
We've talked about why we believe that
divergence uh has developed and what is
likely to cause it to dissipate uh in
our recent Around the Horn weekly Around
the Horn presentations. So, I'll leave
that for for for for those who want to
uh dig in further. Uh the final thing
I'll say is on liquidity, twofold. One,
it is not enough to just know that
liquidity is currently trending higher.
You got to forecast it accurately to
have an informed outlook for asset
markets. And so, the chart on the left
shows the trailing 10-year correlation
of key leading indicators of uh of the
year-over-year rate of change of the
42macro global liquidity proxy. Uh and
the chart on the right shows the
Z-scores of the latest values of those
key leading indicators of the 42macro
global liquidity proxy. And so, based on
the correlations, which we uh apply
three-month uh lead uh three-month lead
and a six-month lead for these uh uh
variables uh relative to their cyclical
three Z-scores and structural Z-scores,
we run an inverse normal distribution
formula on the correlation-adjusted
Z-scores for these key leading
indicators of the global liquidity proxy
relative to uh the uh global liquidity
uh uh the the Z-score for the the global
liquidity proxy itself and subtract the
residual, we see that the uh the
Z-score, the correlation-adjusted
Z-scores for uh these variables are
actually higher on and on a net basis
than the Z-score, the year-over-year
rate of change Z-score for the global
liquidity proxy. So, by that, we
surmise,
based on the size of the residual, that
the it's likely that the global
liquidity has a meaningful uptrend over
the medium term because these leading
indicators, based on their three-month
leading uh correlations and six-month
leading correlations, have all achieved
a level from a rate of change
perspective that implies that global
liquidity needs to catch up to that
level in the coming three to six months.
And so, that's how that's where how we
develop our forecast. So, uh you know,
again, that could be wrong, but at the
end of the day, uh you know, I think the
reason the market's returned to a
risk-on market regime is because this is
true, alongside the fact that our macro
weather model's signaling a a tailwind
for the growth cycle, it's signaling a
tailwind for the monetary policy cycle,
it's signaling a tailwind for the fiscal
policy cycle, and it's signaling a
tailwind for the liquidity cycle. So,
that's just a whole lot of good news uh
for any bear uh to have to fight uh with
respect to the medium-term outlook for
the economy and asset markets. So, uh
we'll wrap it up there. Darius Dale here
presenting our Macro Minute for Tuesday,
April 4th 14th, 2026. Everyone have a
great day out there. We'll catch you
back here tomorrow. Cheers.
